subject: Mathematics
text: The function is said to be one-one (injective) if for all x and y in A, Whenever f(x) = f(y), then x = y And equivalently, if x {tex}\neq{/tex} y, then f(x) {tex}\neq{/tex} f(y)
text_html:
The function is said to be one-one (injective) if for all x and y in A,
Whenever f(x) = f(y), then x = y
And equivalently, if x {tex}\neq{/tex} y, then f(x) {tex}\neq{/tex} f(y)

subject: Mathematics
Mathematics
text: Examples of Injective Function The identity function X {tex}\to{/tex} X is always injective. If function f : R {tex}\to{/tex} R, then f(x) = 2x is injective. If function f : R {tex}\to{/tex} R, then f(x) = 2x + 1 is injective. If function f: R {tex}\to{/tex} R, then f(x) = x 2 is not an injective function, because here if x = -1, then f(-1) = 1 = f(1). Hence, the element of codomain is not discrete here.
text_html:
Examples of Injective Function

  • The identity function X {tex}\to{/tex} X is always injective.
  • If function f : R {tex}\to{/tex} R, then f(x) = 2x is injective.
  • If function f : R {tex}\to{/tex} R, then f(x) = 2x + 1 is injective.
  • If function f: R {tex}\to{/tex} R, then f(x) = x2 is not an injective function, because here if x = -1, then f(-1) = 1 = f(1). Hence, the element of codomain is not discrete here.

subject: Mathematics
text: A function f : X {tex}\to{/tex} Y is said to be onto (or surjective), if every element of Y is the image of some element of X under f, i.e., for every y {tex}\in{/tex} Y, there exists an element x in X such that f(x) = y. Example: The identity function for any set X is an onto function.
text_html:
A function f : X {tex}\to{/tex} Y is said to be onto (or surjective), if every element of Y is the image of some element of X under f, i.e., for every y {tex}\in{/tex} Y, there exists an element x in X such that f(x) = y.
Example: The identity function for any set X is an onto function.

subject: Mathematics
text: Onto Function:
text_html: Onto Function:

subject: Mathematics
text: In the onto function, every element in the codomain will be assigned to at least one value in the domain.
text_html: In the onto function, every element in the codomain will be assigned to at least one value in the domain.

subject: Mathematics
text: A function f : A {tex}\to{/tex} B is an onto, or surjective, function if the range of f equals the codomain of the function f.
text_html: A function f : A {tex}\to{/tex} B is an onto, or surjective, function if the range of f equals the codomain of the function f.

subject: Mathematics
text: Reflexive relation R in X is a relation with (a, a) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\forall{/tex} a {tex}\in{/tex} X.
text_html: Reflexive relation R in X is a relation with (a, a) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\forall{/tex} a {tex}\in{/tex} X.

subject: Mathematics
text: The number of reflexive relations on a set with the ‘n’ number of elements is given by N = 2 n(n-1) , where N is the number of reflexive relations and n is the number of elements in the set.
text_html: The number of reflexive relations on a set with the ‘n’ number of elements is given by N = 2n(n-1), where N is the number of reflexive relations and n is the number of elements in the set.

subject: Mathematics
text: Anti-Reflexive Relation: A relation R defined on a set A is said to be an anti-reflexive relation if no element of A is related to itself. Mathematically, it is represented as (a, a) {tex}\notin{/tex} R for every a {tex}\in{/tex} A. It is also known as irreflexive relation .
text_html: Anti-Reflexive Relation: A relation R defined on a set A is said to be an anti-reflexive relation if no element of A is related to itself. Mathematically, it is represented as (a, a) {tex}\notin{/tex} R for every a {tex}\in{/tex} A. It is also known as irreflexive relation.

subject: Mathematics
text: Symmetric relation R in X is a relation satisfying (a, b) {tex}\in{/tex} R implies (b, a) {tex}\in{/tex} R.
text_html: Symmetric relation R in X is a relation satisfying (a, b) {tex}\in{/tex} R implies (b, a) {tex}\in{/tex} R.

subject: Mathematics
text: Let L be the set of all lines in a plane and R be the relation in L defined as R = {(L 1 , L 2 ) : L 1 is perpendicular to L 2 }. R is symmetric relation.
text_html: Let L be the set of all lines in a plane and R be the relation in L defined as R = {(L1, L2) : L1 is perpendicular to L2}. R is symmetric relation.

subject: Mathematics
text: The number of symmetric relations on a set with the ‘n’ number of elements is given by 2 n(n+1)/2
text_html: The number of symmetric relations on a set with the ‘n’ number of elements is given by 2n(n+1)/2

subject: Mathematics
text: A relation R on a set A is said to be asymmetric if and only if (a, b) {tex}\in{/tex} R, then (b, a) {tex}\notin{/tex} R, for all a, b {tex}\in{/tex} A.
text_html: A relation R on a set A is said to be asymmetric if and only if (a, b) {tex}\in{/tex} R, then (b, a) {tex}\notin{/tex} R, for all a, b {tex}\in{/tex} A.

subject: Mathematics
text: Transitive relation R in X is a relation satisfying (a, b) {tex}\in{/tex} R and (b, c) {tex}\in{/tex} R implies that (a, c) {tex}\in{/tex} R.
text_html: Transitive relation R in X is a relation satisfying (a, b) {tex}\in{/tex} R and (b, c) {tex}\in{/tex} R implies that (a, c) {tex}\in{/tex} R.

subject: Mathematics
text: The relation R in R defined as R = {(a, b) : a {tex}\leq{/tex} b}, is transitive relation.
text_html: The relation R in R defined as R = {(a, b) : a {tex}\leq{/tex} b}, is transitive relation.

subject: Mathematics
text: The union of two transitive relations need not be transitive. For example, Suppose R and S are transitive relations such that (x, y) is in R, and (y, z) is in S, but (x, z) is in neither.
text_html: The union of two transitive relations need not be transitive. For example, Suppose R and S are transitive relations such that (x, y) is in R, and (y, z) is in S, but (x, z) is in neither.

subject: Mathematics
text: The intersection of two transitive relations is a transitive relation. For example, 'is greater than or equal to' and 'is equal to' are transitive relations and their intersection relation is 'is equal to' which is a transitive relation.
text_html: The intersection of two transitive relations is a transitive relation. For example, 'is greater than or equal to' and 'is equal to' are transitive relations and their intersection relation is 'is equal to' which is a transitive relation.

subject: Mathematics
text: A relation defined on an empty set is always a transitive relation.
text_html: A relation defined on an empty set is always a transitive relation.

subject: Mathematics
text: Equivalence relation R in X is a relation which is reflexive, symmetric and transitive.
text_html: Equivalence relation R in X is a relation which is reflexive, symmetric and transitive.

subject: Mathematics
text: Let T be the set of all triangles in a plane with R a relation in T given by R = {(T 1 , T 2 ) : T 1 is congruent to T 2 }. R is an equivalence relation.
text_html: Let T be the set of all triangles in a plane with R a relation in T given by R = {(T1 , T2) : T1 is congruent to T2}. R is an equivalence relation.

subject: Mathematics
text: Equivalence class [a] containing a {tex}\in{/tex} X for an equivalence relation R in X is the subset of X containing all elements b related to a.
text_html: Equivalence class [a] containing a {tex}\in{/tex} X for an equivalence relation R in X is the subset of X containing all elements b related to a.

subject: Mathematics
text: All elements belonging to the same equivalence class are equivalent to each other.
text_html: All elements belonging to the same equivalence class are equivalent to each other.
